Metadiscourse in Master's Thesis Abstracts: Disciplinary Variation and AI Literacy

A living appendix to the thesis: browse the corpus metadata, lexical profiles and metadiscourse-marker results across six disciplinary subcorpora of master's-thesis abstracts deposited at the Universitat Politècnica de València (RiuNet, 2022–2024). Every chart below is rendered live from the source workbook.

Section 1

Sampling population & access

The sampling frame comprises 4,049 TFMs deposited across the six knowledge areas in 2022–2024, split almost evenly into open-access and restricted (closed) theses. From this frame, 30 abstracts per subcorpus were sampled on rhetorical criteria. Abstracts of restricted theses were read from the RiuNet preview; the rest from the final director-approved PDF.

TFM population by access status
Open access Closed (restricted)
Section 2

The corpus at a glance

Each subcorpus concatenates 30 abstracts into a standardised 3,000-word text. METOOL reports types and tokens per subcorpus; the Type–Token Ratio (TTR) indexes lexical density — all six subcorpora fall in the low band (0.319–0.339), confirming comparable lexical texture across disciplines. Token counts exceed 3,000 because METOOL counts reformulation punctuation as tokens.

Lexical profile per subcorpus
Measure

Section 3 · Core results

Textual vs Interpersonal balance

The interactive/interactional split per subcorpus: raw occurrences of Textual (interactive) vs Interpersonal (interactional) markers, with each shown as a share of the discipline's total markers.

By discipline

Raw occurrences of Textual (interactive) vs Interpersonal (interactional) markers per subcorpus. Textual markers dominate every discipline, but the interpersonal share rises in Health, Sciences and Arts & Humanities. Token counts (normalisation base): Architecture 3,338 · Engineering 3,428 · Science & Technology for Health 3,453 · Arts & Humanities 3,400 · Social & Legal Sciences 3,328 · Sciences 3,430. Table cells show raw / % of each discipline's total markers.

Textual (interactive) Interpersonal (interactional)
Full dataset

Interactive data table — raw & normalised

The complete metadiscourse dataset behind every chart: raw occurrences and normalised frequencies (per 1,000 tokens) for all ten categories across the six subcorpora, with corpus totals for every category and marker type.

Metadiscourse occurrences by discipline
Measure

Raw occurrences = the actual count of markers in the subcorpus. Normalised occurrences = (occurrences ÷ subcorpus tokens) × 1,000, so subcorpora of slightly different sizes are directly comparable.

⬇️ Download raw & normalised data (Excel)
Section 4 · Core results

Metadiscourse markers across disciplines

Following Hyland (2005), markers split into Textual (interactive — organising the discourse) and Interpersonal (interactional — projecting stance and engaging the reader), each with five categories. Compare raw occurrences or normalised occurrences (per 1,000 tokens), which control for the small token differences between subcorpora.

Category comparison — all six disciplines
Measure
Marker type

Data-table cells follow the Measure toggle: Normalised shows raw / normalised (per 1,000 tokens); Raw shows raw / % of that discipline's total markers (each discipline = 100%).

    Section 6

    Method notes & limitations

    Disclosed for transparency, drawn directly from the workbook's Data Dictionary and Limitations draft.

    Textual (interactive) — Logical markers, Code Glosses, Frame markers, Endophoric markers, Evidentials. These organise the discourse and guide the reader through the text.

    Interpersonal (interactional) — Boosters, Hedges, Attitude markers, Engagement markers, Self-mentions. These project the writer's stance and involve the reader.

    Markers were annotated with METOOL. Polyfunctional forms (e.g. and, will, such as) are counted once per function under the double-classification protocol, so a form can appear in more than one category.

    Each subcorpus contains 30 abstracts, 10 per deposit year (2022–2024), ~1,000 words per year, selected on rhetorical criteria (lexical bundles signalling Moves 2–4; at least three of five moves per abstract).

    Ten fine-grained subcorpora were originally collected; in consultation with the thesis supervisor the design was consolidated to the six established knowledge areas. Within Engineering & Architecture, only Architecture was kept separate, on the grounds of its distinct, design-oriented rhetorical culture.

    The Health Sciences subcorpus deviates from the balanced 10/10/10 year design (final distribution 10/12/8): 2024 did not offer enough eligible TFMs, so two records were drawn from 2023 to reach the 30-abstract target.

    Because the year groups are unequal, per-year marker rates in this subcorpus cannot be compared on equal terms and the diachronic analysis was postponed. This does not affect the cross-disciplinary comparison, which rests on the complete 3,000-word subcorpora.

    Architecture duplicate. The 2022 record IAAQ7 was inadvertently duplicated into a 2023 slot (+74 non-unique words, +2.5%). Sensitivity analysis: shift <0.2 per 1,000 tokens; no between-discipline comparison affected; the duplicate is excluded from per-abstract and diachronic analyses.

    Source flags. Three abstracts (ENG-20, STH-09, SCI-30) lacked a source flag; all three were confirmed as extracted from the final director-approved TFM PDF and coded accordingly.

    Gender coding. Author gender was coded from given names in the Spanish naming context; 16 records (8.9%), mostly in Arts & Humanities, could not be reliably attributed and were left blank. Any gender comparison is exploratory.

    All 180 abstracts come from a single institutional repository (RiuNet, UPV) and represent novice academic writing in English by L1-Spanish master's students; generalisation requires multi-institutional replication, and expert-corpus comparisons read as novice-vs-expert contrasts.

    With 3,000 words per subcorpus, low-count categories (e.g. endophoric markers, 3–5 occurrences) yield cells too small for inferential treatment and are reported descriptively only. METOOL's automated sense assignment carries an inherent misclassification risk that systematic manual review mitigated but did not eliminate.

    📂
    Full source workbook. Every number on this page is drawn from the appendix workbook (results updated 19 July 2026) — 18 sheets covering abstracts master data, programs availability, corpus summary, the six marker tabs, the data dictionary and the limitations draft.

    Companion workbook · Chapter III → Chapter IV

    The statistics behind the research — and what they say about transferability

    The same study, viewed through its inferential layer. These procedures test whether the disciplinary differences in metadiscourse use are real and, crucially, whether the corpus design itself is transferable — i.e. balanced, evenly dispersed and independently reproducible, so that the same sampling and tagging protocol can be reused on new disciplines and corpora. All inferential statistics were computed with the LLM assistant Kimi (Moonshot AI, 2026) — AI-assisted yet fully traceable, in the spirit of Pérez-Paredes (2026) — and then cross-validated: dispersion against the Lancaster Stats Tools calculator (Brezina, 2018), the standard deviation against the Pearson calculator, and every figure reconciled cell-by-cell against the corpus workbook.

    Kimi · Moonshot AI (2026) · statistics run by LLM Brezina (2018) · method & Lancaster tool Pérez-Paredes (2026) · reproducible, traceable analysis Gries (2008) · DP Juilland & Chang-Rodríguez (1964) · D
    153.57
    Overall χ² (df = 40)
    p < .001 · real disciplinary variation
    .137
    Cramér's V
    small, orderly effect
    .924 / .925
    Macro Juilland's D
    textual / interpersonal — evenly dispersed
    10 / 10
    Lancaster match
    dispersion independently verified
    12.1
    Bayes factor BF₀₁
    stable textual/interpersonal balance
    DATA
    Statistical results · updated 23 July 2026
    Every value on this tab is drawn from the statistical-results workbook (companion to Chapters III–IV). The full, live workbook is available on OneDrive, and a copy can be downloaded from the verification panel below.
    ☁️ Live workbook (OneDrive)
    🧮
    How the statistics were produced. Following Pérez-Paredes's (2026) case for AI-assisted but transparent, reproducible corpus work, all inferential procedures — contingency-table tests, confidence intervals, effect sizes, Monte-Carlo simulation and Bayesian re-analysis — were run in Kimi (Moonshot AI, 2026), the single computation environment for the study. Three independent cross-checks guard every value: dispersion against Lancaster Stats Tools online (Brezina, 2018), the standard deviation against the Pearson calculator, and a cell-by-cell reconciliation against the unified corpus workbook. Metadiscourse tagging used METOOL (Carrió-Pastor, 2020); the statistical method follows Brezina (2018).
    The transferability evidence

    Dispersion — how evenly each category spreads across the six subcorpora

    Transferability rests on dispersion: if a category's occurrences are spread evenly across the six subcorpora rather than concentrated in one, the sampling design generalises. Juilland's D ranges 0 (concentrated) to 1 (perfectly even); DP (deviation of proportions) is the inverse — lower is more even. Both macro-categories reach D ≈ .92, and seven of ten categories sit in the High band (D ≥ .80) — a universal core repertoire — while only the three most discipline-bound categories (frame, endophoric and engagement markers) fall to the Medium band, confirming a balanced, reusable design.

    Juilland's D and DP by category
    Measure
    High (D ≥ .80) Medium (.50–.79) Low (< .50)

    Macro-categories: textual D = .924 (DP .077) · interpersonal D = .925 (DP .073) — both High. Marker level (251 unique forms): mean D = .289 — categories disperse evenly even though individual markers vary, exactly as Brezina (2018) and Gries (2008) predict. Crucially, the evenly-dispersed categories are precisely the ones the independence tests find no difference in, and the concentrated Medium categories are precisely the significant ones — dispersion and significance tell one coherent story, and the design detects difference only where difference exists.

    ✓
    Independently reproduced. All ten category-level Juilland's D values match the verified Lancaster Stats Tools output after two-decimal rounding (10 / 10). Raw Lancaster input and output are archived in the workbook as direct evidence — the reproducibility that Pérez-Paredes (2026) argues corpus work needs.
    ☁️ Live workbook (OneDrive) 🔗 Lancaster dispersion calculator ⬇️ Download the statistical workbook
    Do disciplines really differ?

    Omnibus tests — a real but small, orderly effect

    The whole-table tests are highly significant: disciplines differ in how they use metadiscourse. The effect size is small (Cramér's V = .137), and the textual/interpersonal macro balance does not differ (p = .090) — variation is in the fine categories, not the overall architecture. That combination — real signal, small effect, stable macro-structure — is what makes the design portable.

    Whole-table & macro-level results
    Where the variation lives

    Per-category tests — composition vs rate layer

    Each category is tested twice: in the composition layer (its share of the repertoire) and the rate layer (occurrences per 1,000 tokens), with Bonferroni control (family k = 10). The dashed line marks the χ² ≈ 11.07 critical value (df = 5, α = .05). Frame and logical markers drive the differences in both layers; hedges, attitude markers and self-mentions are stable everywhere.

    χ² by category — composition vs rate
    Composition χ² Rate χ² — — critical χ² (df 5) = 11.07
    Which cells drive the table

    Driver cells — adjusted standardised residuals

    Adjusted standardised residuals (ASR) locate the category × discipline cells responsible for the significant table. Positive = over-use, negative = under-use. Cells beyond |z| = 3.31 survive the strict family-wise (Bonferroni) threshold over all 54 cells; the rest hold at the per-comparison level (|z| > 2.58).

    ASR by driver cell
    Over-use (+) Under-use (−)
    How big are the differences

    Key pairwise contrasts — effect sizes

    The strongest rate contrasts, with Log Ratio (log₂) effect sizes and %DIFF. A Log Ratio of 2.41 means the higher rate is 22.41 ≈ 5.3× the lower one. Each rate carries a 95% Wilson confidence interval (see the table).

    Log Ratio by contrast
    Robustness checks

    No soft/hard divide · between-subcorpus spread

    Grouping the disciplines into "soft" and "hard" science yields no detectable difference in the textual/interpersonal balance — and Bayes factors quantify positive evidence for that null (the data are ≈ 12× more likely under "no divide"). The coefficient of variation across subcorpora is modest (≈ 17–18%), another marker of a balanced design.

    Soft vs hard science
    Between-subcorpus descriptives

    Statistics over the six subcorpus subtotals of normalised occurrences per 1,000 tokens (n = 6 subcorpora × 30 abstracts = 180 texts).

    Full method record

    Every procedure, what it measures, and the result

    The master table maps each statistical procedure to what it tests, the result obtained, and where it is reported in the thesis — a transparent, auditable chain from method to claim.

    References & methodology

    Corpus-linguistics research methodology

    The design and its transferability rest on an established corpus-statistics methodology (Brezina, 2018; Gries, 2008; Juilland & Chang-Rodríguez, 1964); the reproducibility argument follows Pérez-Paredes's (2026) open-box, traceable-evidence view of corpus work. All statistics were computed with Kimi (Moonshot AI, 2026) in Python and independently cross-checked. The full bibliography (APA 7th, with DOIs and links) and the declaration of AI use are on the References tab.

    Complete references, tools and the AI-use declaration →

    Transparency · Bibliography · APA 7th

    References & declaration of AI use

    The complete works cited across the thesis, in APA 7th edition with DOIs and open-access links, together with a full disclosure of the AI tools used to compute and present the statistics — in line with Pérez-Paredes's (2026) call for transparent, reproducible, traceable corpus work. The canonical list is the thesis's own References chapter; entries here are compiled from the in-text citations of Chapters I–V.

    How to cite

    Citing this data book

    📑

    APA 7th

    Eslava-Graterol, A. (2026). Interactive corpus data book: Metadiscourse in master's thesis abstracts — disciplinary variation and AI literacy [Data set]. https://profeanaeslavaedtech.github.io/portofolio/metadiscourse-visualization.html

    Declaration

    Declaration of AI use

    🤖

    Statistical computation. All inferential statistics reported in this study were computed with Kimi (Moonshot AI, 2026), an AI computational assistant used as the statistical-analysis environment, which acted solely as a creator and executor of Python 3 code for every procedure — normalisation, contingency-table tests (χ² and log-likelihood), Cramér's V and Log-Ratio effect sizes, Wilson and Newcombe confidence intervals, Monte-Carlo simulation, Bayesian (Bayes-factor) re-analysis, and the dispersion measures (DP, Juilland's D). The tool ran under short, fully specified instructions naming the test, formula, input matrix and output format; every analytical decision — which tests to run, which significance thresholds and corrections to apply — was taken by the author and fixed in advance. This AI-assisted workflow follows Pérez-Paredes's (2026) argument that AI can support corpus analysis provided the process stays transparent and reproducible.

    Independent verification. Every value was cross-checked by non-AI means: dispersion against the Lancaster Stats Tools online calculator (Brezina, 2018) — all ten categories matched — the standard deviation against the Pearson online calculator, and a cell-by-cell reconciliation against the unified corpus workbook. No result enters the thesis unless it reproduces exactly from the raw marker counts.

    Human authorship. The research questions, corpus design and selection, metadiscourse annotation decisions, interpretation of the results, and the writing of the thesis are the author's own. AI was used as a computational and tagging aid, not to generate findings or arguments. Corpus tagging used METOOL (Carrió-Pastor, 2020); the animated results overview was produced with a motion-graphics AI tool (Easy Motion AI, 2026); this interactive data book was built with an AI coding assistant. All uses are disclosed here for full transparency and responsible, ethical Human–AI collaboration.

    Developed within the framework of the master's thesis
    "Metadiscourse in Master's Thesis Abstracts: Disciplinary Variation and AI Literacy"
    by Prof. Mª Luisa Carrió-Pastor and Prof. Ana Eslava-Graterol · Department of Applied Linguistics · Universitat Politècnica de València (UPV).

    Interactive data book built with Chart.js. Metadiscourse markers follow Hyland (2005) as annotated in METOOL; occurrences normalised per 1,000 tokens. Chart palette validated for colour-vision-deficiency accessibility. Charts reflect the corpus results updated 19 July 2026. A project in ethical, transparent Human–AI collaboration for AI literacy and academic writing.
